chunk_summary: This section discusses enteral and parenteral nutrition strategies for pediatric patients with intestinal failure and short bowel syndrome, focusing on proactive PN escalation, electrolyte management, and long-term complication prevention.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for escalating to parenteral nutrition in pediatric intestinal failure.
Learn strategies to optimize enteral feeding in cases of malabsorption and high-output losses.
Recognize the importance of monitoring and preventing long-term PN complications such as cholestasis and metabolic bone disease.
Identify the role of specialized intestinal rehabilitation teams in managing complex cases.
key_facts: High-output fluid losses and protein-energy underdelivery are dominant risks in intestinal failure.
Stool or stoma output should be monitored in mL/kg/day to guide enteral feeding advancement.
Electrolyte prescriptions should replace measured gastrointestinal losses, especially sodium, and include higher zinc dosing when outputs are high.
Proactive PN escalation is recommended if enteral targets cannot be met due to persistent high output, dehydration, or inability to advance beyond trophic volumes.
Long-term venous access requires structured monitoring for complications like cholestasis and metabolic bone disease.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Electrolyte prescription should explicitly replace measured gastrointestinal losses (especially sodium) and include higher zinc dosing and monitoring when outputs are high.
document_key_topics: Enteral and parenteral nutrition in pediatric intestinal failure
Criteria for proactive parenteral nutrition escalation
Management of high-output fluid and electrolyte losses
Prevention of long-term parenteral nutrition complications
